title: Acromegaly
oneLiner: Chronic GH excess (usually pituitary adenoma) causing progressive disfigurement, cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, and premature cardiovascular death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pituitary apoplexy:** sudden severe headache + visual field defect + ptosis/ophthalmoplegia + hypotension/hypertension. *Key finding:* abrupt onset of retro-orbital headache with any cranial nerve palsy.
- **Severe obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) with respiratory failure:** hypoxia, hypercapnia, cor pulmonale. *Key finding:* acute type 2 respiratory failure (high pCO₂) on ABG + heavy snoring history.
- **Hypertensive emergency:** refractory hypertension with end-organ damage (LVF, hypertensive encephalopathy, intracranial haemorrhage) due to GH-induced sodium retention. *Key finding:* BP >220/120 with papilloedema or pulmonary oedema.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** coarse facial features (enlarged nose, lips, jaw; prognathism, macroglossia); enlarging hands/feet (ring, glove, shoe size change); excessive sweating (hyperhidrosis); headache; joint pain; fatigue; snoring/sleep apnoea; carpal tunnel syndrome; galactorrhoea (if prolactin co-secreted); oligomenorrhoea/erectile dysfunction; weight gain despite normal diet.
- **Key exam findings:** prognathism, macroglossia, wide philtrum, prominent forehead; thickened palms, broad digits (spade‑like hands); hypertension; diastolic murmur (valvular disease); skin tags; goitre features of diabetes (obesity, acanthosis nigricans); visual field deficit (bitemporal hemianopia).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Pseudoacromegaly** (severe insulin resistance / diabetes / lipodystrophy): no elevated GH/IGF‑1.
- **Gigantism** (onset before epiphyseal fusion): tall stature >acral enlargement.
- **Primary hypogonadism:** some acral features but normal GH/IGF‑1.
- **Thyroid acropachy** (Graves’ disease): clubbing, periosteal new bone but no GH excess.
- **Splitter:** Elevated age‑adjusted IGF‑1 is diagnostic; if equivocal, OGTT with GH suppression (see Investigations).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** visual fields by confrontation; BP (seated/standing); ECG (LVH, conduction defects); oxygen saturation; urine dip (glycosuria).
- **Bloods:** IGF‑1 (first‑line screening – interpret by age/sex normal ranges); GH (random not reliable; fasting GH may be elevated but OGTT is confirmatory); prolactin; pituitary panel (TSH, free T4, cortisol/ACTH, LH, FSH, testosterone/oestradiol); U&Es, calcium (hypercalciuria); HbA1c; lipids.
- **Imaging:**
  - First‑line: MRI pituitary with gadolinium (identify adenoma ± cavernous sinus invasion).
  - Second‑line: CT head if MRI contraindicated; CT chest/abdomen if ectopic GHRH suspected (very rare).
- **Special tests:** Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) – give 75g glucose; measure GH at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120 min. Failure of GH suppression to <1 ng/mL confirms acromegaly. Also used post‑surgery/monitoring.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE: elevated age‑related IGF‑1 + failure of GH to suppress <1 ng/mL on OGTT. MRI evidence of adenoma.
- **Never‑miss test:** Pituitary MRI with contrast (pituitary apoplexy can be missed without MRI). Also baseline ECG and echocardiogram (silent cardiomyopathy is common).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Suspected apoplexy: IV hydrocortisone 100mg stat + fluid resuscitation (normal saline) + urgent MRI + neurosurgery referral.
  - Hypertensive crisis: IV labetalol or GTN per local protocol.
  - Respiratory failure from OSA: oxygen, consider NIV (CPAP if known OSA).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with IGF‑1 plus OGTT.
  - Start medical therapy if surgical wait >4 weeks or if patient has severe symptoms/comorbidities.
  - Monitor for complications: 4‑hourly neuro‑obs; strict fluid balance and urine output (detect diabetes insipidus post‑op); daily U&Es; blood glucose (sliding scale if needed); BP control.
  - Pre‑operative assessment: sleep study, cardiac echo, anaesthetic review.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to specialist pituitary MDT (endocrinology + neurosurgery).
  - First‑line definitive treatment: transsphenoidal adenomectomy (microadenomas ~80–90% remission; macroadenomas ~50–60%).
  - Medical therapy (if surgery contraindicated/failed): somatostatin analogues (octreotide / lanreotide) or GH receptor antagonist (pegvisomant).
  - Radiotherapy for residual disease (delayed effect; risk of hypopituitarism).
  - Manage comorbidities: antihypertensives, antidiabetic agents, CPAP for OSA.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline at maintenance if NBM for surgery.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) if no contraindication.
  - Diet: normal unless NBM.
  - Observations: 4‑hourly vital signs + neuro‑obs if pituitary lesion.
  - Strict fluid balance and urine output chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents (somatostatin analogues):**
  - Octreotide SC: start 50–100mcg three times daily; long‑acting IM: 20–30mg every 4 weeks.
  - Lanreotide SC: 60–120mg every 4 weeks (dose per local formulary).
  - *Dose adjustments based on GH/IGF‑1 response – check local policy.*
- **Alternatives:**
  - Pasireotide SC twice daily then monthly IM (second‑line; higher risk of hyperglycaemia).
  - Pegvisomant SC once daily: start 10–20mg, then titrate up to [check local max] (monitor liver enzymes).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Octreotide can cause gallstones, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia/hyperglycaemia, hypothyroidism.
  - Pegvisomant contraindicated in hepatic impairment (severe).
  - Corticosteroid use in apoplexy: hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat, then [check local stress dose protocol].
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Octreotide: caution in renal impairment (reduce dose if GFR <30).
  - Pegvisomant: avoid if hepatic dysfunction.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Giving long‑acting octreotide depot without first testing tolerance with SC short‑acting (risk of severe bradycardia, gallstone pancreatitis).
  - Missing the initial hydrocortisone bolus in pituitary apoplexy (can be fatal).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Pituitary apoplexy, hypertensive crisis, acute heart failure, type 2 respiratory failure (OSA), post‑operative CSF leak (with risk of meningitis), diabetes insipidus (transient or permanent).
- **Chronic:** Cardiomyopathy (concentric LVH, diastolic dysfunction), valvular heart disease, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, obstructive sleep apnoea, arthropathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, colonic polyps (increased colorectal cancer risk), hypopituitarism (post‑surgery/RT).
sections.Prognosis: - Treated patients with normalised GH/IGF‑1 have near‑normal life expectancy (cardiovascular risk reverses early). Untreated, mortality is 2–4x higher (mainly cardiovascular). Life expectancy reduced by ~10 years without therapy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check IGF‑1 + consider OGTT** when acromegaly is suspected – early diagnosis prevents irreversible changes.
- **Assess for sleep apnoea in all patients** – start CPAP to reduce cardiovascular risk.
- **Perform baseline echocardiogram + 24‑hour BP monitoring** – even if asymptomatic – due to high prevalence of subclinical cardiomyopathy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume acromegaly is only a cosmetic issue** – it is a systemic disease with high cardiovascular mortality.
- **Do not start medical therapy without confirming diagnosis with OGTT** – pseudoacromegaly can mimic.
- **Do not forget to screen for colonic polyps** – start colonoscopy at diagnosis (age ~40 or at diagnosis), repeat every 3–5 years.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New severe headache, visual disturbance, or weakness (apoplexy); worsening breathlessness or chest pain; severe hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia (if on somatostatin analogues or pegvisomant).
- **Follow‑up:** Endocrine clinic within 4–6 weeks for results and treatment plan; if post‑op, early post‑operative endocrinology review (within 2 weeks).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call ICU / crash team if: NEWS2 ≥6 or rising; GCS <15 with signs of raised ICP (apoplexy); new focal neurology; type 2 respiratory failure; refractory hypertension with end‑organ damage.
- Call senior endocrinologist / neurosurgery urgently if: pituitary apoplexy suspected; post‑operative CSF leak; severe electrolyte disturbance (hyponatraemia or hypernatraemia from DI).

rawMarkdown:
# Acromegaly
**One-liner:** Chronic GH excess (usually pituitary adenoma) causing progressive disfigurement, cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, and premature cardiovascular death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pituitary apoplexy:** sudden severe headache + visual field defect + ptosis/ophthalmoplegia + hypotension/hypertension. *Key finding:* abrupt onset of retro-orbital headache with any cranial nerve palsy.
- **Severe obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) with respiratory failure:** hypoxia, hypercapnia, cor pulmonale. *Key finding:* acute type 2 respiratory failure (high pCO₂) on ABG + heavy snoring history.
- **Hypertensive emergency:** refractory hypertension with end-organ damage (LVF, hypertensive encephalopathy, intracranial haemorrhage) due to GH-induced sodium retention. *Key finding:* BP >220/120 with papilloedema or pulmonary oedema.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** coarse facial features (enlarged nose, lips, jaw; prognathism, macroglossia); enlarging hands/feet (ring, glove, shoe size change); excessive sweating (hyperhidrosis); headache; joint pain; fatigue; snoring/sleep apnoea; carpal tunnel syndrome; galactorrhoea (if prolactin co-secreted); oligomenorrhoea/erectile dysfunction; weight gain despite normal diet.
- **Key exam findings:** prognathism, macroglossia, wide philtrum, prominent forehead; thickened palms, broad digits (spade‑like hands); hypertension; diastolic murmur (valvular disease); skin tags; goitre features of diabetes (obesity, acanthosis nigricans); visual field deficit (bitemporal hemianopia).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Pseudoacromegaly** (severe insulin resistance / diabetes / lipodystrophy): no elevated GH/IGF‑1.
- **Gigantism** (onset before epiphyseal fusion): tall stature >acral enlargement.
- **Primary hypogonadism:** some acral features but normal GH/IGF‑1.
- **Thyroid acropachy** (Graves’ disease): clubbing, periosteal new bone but no GH excess.
- **Splitter:** Elevated age‑adjusted IGF‑1 is diagnostic; if equivocal, OGTT with GH suppression (see Investigations).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** visual fields by confrontation; BP (seated/standing); ECG (LVH, conduction defects); oxygen saturation; urine dip (glycosuria).
- **Bloods:** IGF‑1 (first‑line screening – interpret by age/sex normal ranges); GH (random not reliable; fasting GH may be elevated but OGTT is confirmatory); prolactin; pituitary panel (TSH, free T4, cortisol/ACTH, LH, FSH, testosterone/oestradiol); U&Es, calcium (hypercalciuria); HbA1c; lipids.
- **Imaging:**
  - First‑line: MRI pituitary with gadolinium (identify adenoma ± cavernous sinus invasion).
  - Second‑line: CT head if MRI contraindicated; CT chest/abdomen if ectopic GHRH suspected (very rare).
- **Special tests:** Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) – give 75g glucose; measure GH at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120 min. Failure of GH suppression to <1 ng/mL confirms acromegaly. Also used post‑surgery/monitoring.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE: elevated age‑related IGF‑1 + failure of GH to suppress <1 ng/mL on OGTT. MRI evidence of adenoma.
- **Never‑miss test:** Pituitary MRI with contrast (pituitary apoplexy can be missed without MRI). Also baseline ECG and echocardiogram (silent cardiomyopathy is common).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Suspected apoplexy: IV hydrocortisone 100mg stat + fluid resuscitation (normal saline) + urgent MRI + neurosurgery referral.
  - Hypertensive crisis: IV labetalol or GTN per local protocol.
  - Respiratory failure from OSA: oxygen, consider NIV (CPAP if known OSA).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with IGF‑1 plus OGTT.
  - Start medical therapy if surgical wait >4 weeks or if patient has severe symptoms/comorbidities.
  - Monitor for complications: 4‑hourly neuro‑obs; strict fluid balance and urine output (detect diabetes insipidus post‑op); daily U&Es; blood glucose (sliding scale if needed); BP control.
  - Pre‑operative assessment: sleep study, cardiac echo, anaesthetic review.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to specialist pituitary MDT (endocrinology + neurosurgery).
  - First‑line definitive treatment: transsphenoidal adenomectomy (microadenomas ~80–90% remission; macroadenomas ~50–60%).
  - Medical therapy (if surgery contraindicated/failed): somatostatin analogues (octreotide / lanreotide) or GH receptor antagonist (pegvisomant).
  - Radiotherapy for residual disease (delayed effect; risk of hypopituitarism).
  - Manage comorbidities: antihypertensives, antidiabetic agents, CPAP for OSA.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline at maintenance if NBM for surgery.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) if no contraindication.
  - Diet: normal unless NBM.
  - Observations: 4‑hourly vital signs + neuro‑obs if pituitary lesion.
  - Strict fluid balance and urine output chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents (somatostatin analogues):**
  - Octreotide SC: start 50–100mcg three times daily; long‑acting IM: 20–30mg every 4 weeks.
  - Lanreotide SC: 60–120mg every 4 weeks (dose per local formulary).
  - *Dose adjustments based on GH/IGF‑1 response – check local policy.*
- **Alternatives:**
  - Pasireotide SC twice daily then monthly IM (second‑line; higher risk of hyperglycaemia).
  - Pegvisomant SC once daily: start 10–20mg, then titrate up to [check local max] (monitor liver enzymes).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Octreotide can cause gallstones, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia/hyperglycaemia, hypothyroidism.
  - Pegvisomant contraindicated in hepatic impairment (severe).
  - Corticosteroid use in apoplexy: hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat, then [check local stress dose protocol].
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Octreotide: caution in renal impairment (reduce dose if GFR <30).
  - Pegvisomant: avoid if hepatic dysfunction.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Giving long‑acting octreotide depot without first testing tolerance with SC short‑acting (risk of severe bradycardia, gallstone pancreatitis).
  - Missing the initial hydrocortisone bolus in pituitary apoplexy (can be fatal).

### Complications
- **Acute:** Pituitary apoplexy, hypertensive crisis, acute heart failure, type 2 respiratory failure (OSA), post‑operative CSF leak (with risk of meningitis), diabetes insipidus (transient or permanent).
- **Chronic:** Cardiomyopathy (concentric LVH, diastolic dysfunction), valvular heart disease, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, obstructive sleep apnoea, arthropathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, colonic polyps (increased colorectal cancer risk), hypopituitarism (post‑surgery/RT).

### Prognosis
- Treated patients with normalised GH/IGF‑1 have near‑normal life expectancy (cardiovascular risk reverses early). Untreated, mortality is 2–4x higher (mainly cardiovascular). Life expectancy reduced by ~10 years without therapy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check IGF‑1 + consider OGTT** when acromegaly is suspected – early diagnosis prevents irreversible changes.
- **Assess for sleep apnoea in all patients** – start CPAP to reduce cardiovascular risk.
- **Perform baseline echocardiogram + 24‑hour BP monitoring** – even if asymptomatic – due to high prevalence of subclinical cardiomyopathy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume acromegaly is only a cosmetic issue** – it is a systemic disease with high cardiovascular mortality.
- **Do not start medical therapy without confirming diagnosis with OGTT** – pseudoacromegaly can mimic.
- **Do not forget to screen for colonic polyps** – start colonoscopy at diagnosis (age ~40 or at diagnosis), repeat every 3–5 years.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New severe headache, visual disturbance, or weakness (apoplexy); worsening breathlessness or chest pain; severe hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia (if on somatostatin analogues or pegvisomant).
- **Follow‑up:** Endocrine clinic within 4–6 weeks for results and treatment plan; if post‑op, early post‑operative endocrinology review (within 2 weeks).

### Escalation Criteria
- Call ICU / crash team if: NEWS2 ≥6 or rising; GCS <15 with signs of raised ICP (apoplexy); new focal neurology; type 2 respiratory failure; refractory hypertension with end‑organ damage.
- Call senior endocrinologist / neurosurgery urgently if: pituitary apoplexy suspected; post‑operative CSF leak; severe electrolyte disturbance (hyponatraemia or hypernatraemia from DI).
